# UC-22.8.19 · SOC 2 CC6 — Timeliness of Access Removal After HR Termination Events

## Description

CC6 expects removal of access when no longer required. Measuring lag between HR termination timestamp and last successful disablement event across IdM and key SaaS apps proves offboarding SLAs are met—high-risk evidence for every SOC examination.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for SOC 2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes hr, identity and sourcetypes okta:system, workday:termination. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Align `employee_id` across HR and IdM; (2) tune 120 minutes to contractual SLA; (3) add parallel tracks for AD disable and VPN revoke; (4) page IAM on breach; (5) monthly median lag KPI for management review. Indexes required: `index=hr`, `index=identity`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=okta:system`, `sourcetype=workday:termination`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Workday/Okta logs via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=hr` `sourcetype="workday:termination"` (employee_id, term_effective_ts); `index=identity` `sourcetype="okta:system"` (eventType, target_user_id, published).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Align `employee_id` across HR and IdM; (2) tune 120 minutes to contractual SLA; (3) add parallel tracks for AD disable and VPN revoke; (4) page IAM on breach; (5) monthly median lag KPI for management review.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=hr sourcetype="workday:termination" earliest=-30d
| eval term=strptime(term_effective_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left employee_id [
    search index=identity sourcetype="okta:system" eventType="user.lifecycle.deactivate" earliest=-30d
    | eval deactivated=strptime(published,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(deactivated) as first_deact by target_user_id
    | rename target_user_id as employee_id
  ]
| eval lag_sec=first_deact-term
| eval lag_min=round(lag_sec/60,1)
| where lag_min>120 OR isnull(first_deact)
| table employee_id, term, first_deact, lag_min
```

Understanding this SPL

**SOC 2 CC6 — Timeliness of Access Removal After HR Termination Events** — CC6 expects removal of access when no longer required. Measuring lag between HR termination timestamp and last successful disablement event across IdM and key SaaS apps proves offboarding SLAs are met—high-risk evidence for every SOC examination.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=hr` `sourcetype="workday:termination"` (employee_id, term_effective_ts); `index=identity` `sourcetype="okta:system"` (eventType, target_user_id, published). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Workday/Okta logs via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: hr; **sourcetype**: workday:termination. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=hr, sourcetype="workday:termination", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **term** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Correlates data from a subsearch to enrich the primary result set with additional context.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **lag_sec** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **lag_min** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where lag_min>120 OR isnull(first_deact)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**SOC 2 CC6 — Timeliness of Access Removal After HR Termination Events** — CC6 expects removal of access when no longer required. Measuring lag between HR termination timestamp and last successful disablement event across IdM and key SaaS apps proves offboarding SLAs are met—high-risk evidence for every SOC examination.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=hr` `sourcetype="workday:termination"` (employee_id, term_effective_ts); `index=identity` `sourcetype="okta:system"` (eventType, target_user_id, published). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase 742), Workday/Okta logs via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: deactivated, employee_id, first_deact, lag_min, lag_sec, term.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the asset/access review evidence. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (breaches), Histogram (lag_min), Single value (p95 lag).

## SPL

```spl
index=hr sourcetype="workday:termination" earliest=-30d
| eval term=strptime(term_effective_ts,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| join type=left employee_id [
    search index=identity sourcetype="okta:system" eventType="user.lifecycle.deactivate" earliest=-30d
    | eval deactivated=strptime(published,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    | stats min(deactivated) as first_deact by target_user_id
    | rename target_user_id as employee_id
  ]
| eval lag_sec=first_deact-term
| eval lag_min=round(lag_sec/60,1)
| where lag_min>120 OR isnull(first_deact)
| table employee_id, term, first_deact, lag_min
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Table (breaches), Histogram (lag_min), Single value (p95 lag).
